pattern: Because every step must strictly increase, the grid has no cycles, so the longest strictly increasing path from each cell is a memoized depth-first search over a directed acyclic graph.
hints: A path can only move to a strictly larger neighbor, which means you can never revisit a cell, so there is no cycle to worry about.
The best path starting at a cell depends only on the best paths starting at its larger neighbors, so the answer for each cell can be cached.
Run a depth-first search from each cell that recurses into strictly larger neighbors, memoize the longest length found at each cell, and return the maximum over all cells.

edge_inputs.description: a small grid with a four-long increasing path
a grid whose longest path winds through several rows
single cell has a path of length one
all equal values means no move is allowed, so the answer is one
a single row strictly increasing left to right
a snake-shaped strictly increasing path that must turn
single column strictly decreasing top to bottom

stress.description: random grids with a narrow value range so many equal neighbors block movement
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    side = max(1, min(size, 40))
    rows = rng.randint(1, side)
    cols = rng.randint(1, side)
    cap = max(2, (rows * cols) // 2)
    return [[[rng.randint(0, cap) for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]]
stress.note: narrow range yields ties that break naive paths

stress.description: larger random grids with a wide value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    side = max(1, min(size, 60))
    rows = rng.randint(1, side)
    cols = rng.randint(1, side)
    return [[[rng.randint(0, 1000000) for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]]
